fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a CSV conversion library built out — something devs can drop in to handle reading and writing tabular data without rolling their own brittle parsers every time. The big pain points we keep hearing from users: data gets corrupted when fields have commas or newlines in them, spreadsheet files don't open cleanly in Excel because of encoding weirdness, and pipelines that receive data in chunks keep breaking mid-stream because the parser can't stitch things back together. We want both directions covered (writing rows to text AND reading text back into rows), with sensible defaults but enough knobs to handle the weird stuff.

One thing — make sure the quoting behavior works like we did in the formatting module from the analytics pipeline (you'll know it when you see it), specifically around how special characters inside fields get handled. Also need it to work with those spreadsheet hint lines that some exports spit out at the top.

On the decoding side, it'd be great if numbers and booleans came back as actual typed values instead of strings when the caller wants that. Validation upfront would also save a lot of runtime surprises. The output format for the adapter layer needs to be consistent — ask me if you're unsure what the rendering looks like for headers vs plain rows. Multi-file structure please, this isn't a quick script.

trigger_keywords: default delimiter
what separator is used by default
which field delimiter if none specified
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The default field delimiter is a comma (`,`). The default line delimiter is carriage-return plus line-feed (`\r\n`). These apply to both encoding and decoding unless overridden in config.

trigger_keywords: quoting policy default
when are fields quoted
necessary quote mode behavior
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The default quoting policy is `necessary`: a field is wrapped in the quote character only when it contains the field delimiter, a line break, or the quote character itself. There are two other explicit modes: `always` (quote every field regardless) and `strings` (quote only textual/string-typed fields, leave numbers and booleans unquoted).

trigger_keywords: escape embedded quote
how are quotes inside fields escaped
doubling quotes vs escape character
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: By default the escape character equals the quote character (`"`), so an embedded quote is escaped by doubling it (e.g., `""` inside a quoted field represents a single `"`). If a custom escape character is configured (e.g., `\`), the escape character is placed before the embedded quote instead of doubling it (e.g., `\"`).

trigger_keywords: excel preset
spreadsheet compatible output
BOM semicolon encoding
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `excel` preset sets the field delimiter to semicolon (`;`) and prepends a UTF-8 byte-order mark (`\uFEFF`) to the encoded output. The default necessary-quoting policy still applies, so a field containing a semicolon will be quoted.

trigger_keywords: sep hint line
delimiter hint preamble
sep= at top of file
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the input begins with a line of the form `sep=<char>` (e.g., `sep=;`), that character is used as the field delimiter for the rest of the document. The hint line itself is consumed and never emitted as a data row. This takes precedence over auto-detection.

trigger_keywords: auto detect delimiter
infer separator
autoDetect config option
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Delimiter auto-detection is ON by default. When enabled, the decoder examines common candidates — comma, semicolon, tab, pipe — and picks the one that most consistently separates fields across the leading lines. Auto-detection can be disabled by setting `autoDetect: false` in config, in which case the configured `fieldDelimiter` (defaulting to comma) is used.

trigger_keywords: dynamic typing
coerce fields to numbers
typed values from decoded fields
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When `dynamicTyping: true` is set in decoder config, each field is examined after parsing and converted to `int`, `double`, or `bool` if its full text exactly matches that form. Boolean recognition is exact and case-sensitive (`true`/`false` only). Even quoted fields are subject to typing. Fields that don't match stay as `str`. The type tag in output changes accordingly: `int=`, `double=`, `bool=`, or `str=`.

trigger_keywords: header row parsing
decode_headers operation
first row as column names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Use the `decode_headers` operation (not `decode`) to treat the first row as a header row. The output reports only data rows (not the header row itself), and each field is rendered as `"<header>": <tag>=<value>` in column order. The `rows=<n>` count reflects only data rows, not the header row.

trigger_keywords: skip empty lines
blank row handling
skipEmptyLines config
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: `skipEmptyLines` is `true` by default. With it enabled, rows consisting solely of empty fields are dropped. With it set to `false`, every physical line including blank ones becomes a row in the output. Note: a single trailing line break after the last row does NOT create an extra empty row regardless of this setting.

trigger_keywords: chunked decoding
streaming decode
decode_chunks operation
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `decode_chunks` operation accepts an array of string chunks in `chunks` field instead of a single `input` string. The decoder buffers partial content across chunk boundaries and correctly reconstructs rows even when field delimiters, line terminators, escape sequences, or multi-character delimiters are split across chunks. Output format is the same as `decode`: `rows=<n>` then `row[i]: ...`.

trigger_keywords: batch decoding
decode_batches operation
maxRowsPerBatch
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `decode_batches` operation works like `decode_chunks` but groups decoded rows into batches. Without `maxRowsPerBatch`, each chunk produces one batch containing all rows decoded from that chunk. With `maxRowsPerBatch: N`, a single chunk's rows are split into batches of at most N rows. Output format: `batches=<n>` then `batch[i] rows=<k>: [ <row> ] [ <row> ] ...` where each row is rendered as `<tag>=<json> | ...`.

trigger_keywords: validate decoder config
validate_decoder operation
quote character validation
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
The `validate_decoder` operation checks configuration up front. The `quoteCharacter` and `escapeCharacter` must each be exactly one character. Supplying a multi-character value for `quoteCharacter` returns `error=invalid_quote_character
`; for `escapeCharacter` returns `error=invalid_escape_character
`. A valid config returns `valid=true
`.

trigger_keywords: streaming encode
encode_stream operation
incremental row encoding
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `encode_stream` operation takes `rows` (array of arrays) and encodes them incrementally. The first row contributes no leading line delimiter; each subsequent row is prefixed by the line delimiter. The concatenation of all fragments equals the full CSV text. Output is the raw CSV text (same as `encode`).

trigger_keywords: output rendering format
how fields are printed in decode output
tag=json field format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each decoded field is rendered as `<tag>=<json>` where tag is `str`, `int`, `double`, or `bool`, and json is the JSON encoding of the value. String fields appear with JSON quoting (e.g., `str="hello"`), numbers appear unquoted (e.g., `int=123`, `double=1.5`), booleans appear as `bool=true` or `bool=false`. Fields within a row are separated by ` | `. Each row line ends with a newline. The output always ends with a trailing newline after the last row line.

trigger_keywords: multi-character delimiter
fieldDelimiter more than one char
split multi-char delimiter across chunks
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `fieldDelimiter` config option supports multi-character strings (e.g., `::`). The entire sequence must be present to separate fields; a partial match (e.g., a single `:` when delimiter is `::`) is treated as ordinary content. In chunked decoding, a multi-character delimiter split across a chunk boundary is correctly buffered and rejoined. Auto-detection must be disabled (`autoDetect: false`) when using a multi-character delimiter.

trigger_keywords: CRLF split across chunks
carriage return line feed boundary
split line terminator in streaming
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A `\r\n` line terminator that is split across a chunk boundary (i.e., `\r` ends one chunk and `\n` starts the next) must be recognized as a single line terminator — not as two separate events. This means no spurious empty row is produced from the split, regardless of the `skipEmptyLines` setting.

trigger_keywords: BOM stripping
byte order mark
UTF-8 BOM in input
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A UTF-8 byte-order mark (`\uFEFF`) at the very start of the input is consumed and never becomes part of the first field's value. This applies whether the delimiter is supplied explicitly or inferred via auto-detection, and also in chunked/streaming decode modes.

trigger_keywords: addBom config encode
prepend BOM on encoding
byte order mark in encoded output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: On the encoding side, setting `addBom: true` in config causes a UTF-8 byte-order mark (`\uFEFF`) to be prepended to the encoded output. The `excel` preset implicitly sets this. By default `addBom` is `false`.

trigger_keywords: decode_split_scan operation
split scan all positions
verify every chunk split point
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `decode_split_scan` operation takes a single `input` string and splits it at every possible position (0 through len), feeding each split as two chunks to the decoder, and verifies correctness at each split point. Output format: `len=<N>` then `split[i]: [ <row fields> ]` for each split position 0..N inclusive. This is used to verify robustness of chunked decoding at all boundary positions.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what values can op take
list all supported op values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'decode', 'decode_headers', 'decode_chunks', 'decode_split_scan', 'encode', 'encode_stream', 'decode_batches', 'validate_decoder'. Each value maps to one feature.

Here are the public test cases for feature19_small_chunk_detect:
{
  "description": "Decode CSV text streamed one character at a time, forcing delimiter detection and the spreadsheet delimiter hint to work under maximal fragmentation. Even when no chunk individually contains enough context, the decoder buffers until it can determine the delimiter and then yields the same rows as a single-shot decode.",

          "e",
          "p",

          "\r",
          "\n",
          "a",
          ";",
          "b",
          ";",
          "c"

    },

        "op": "decode_chunks",
        "chunks": [
          "a",
          ",",
          "b",
          ",",
          "c",
          "\n",
          "1",
          ",",
          "2",
          ",",
          "3"
        ]
      },
      "expected_output": "rows=2\nrow[0]: str=\"a\" | str=\"b\" | str=\"c\"\nrow[1]: str=\"1\" | str=\"2\" | str=\"3\"\n"
    }
  ]
}

context_pointers.vague_reference: the formatting module from the analytics pipeline
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The quoting and escape logic in the CSV encoder: specifically the `necessary` quoting policy (quote only when field contains delimiter, line break, or quote char) and the escape-by-doubling behavior defined in feature1_2/feature2_encode_quoting and feature4_encode_escape_char test cases

context_pointers.vague_reference: those spreadsheet hint lines that some exports spit out at the top
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The `sep=<char>` delimiter hint preamble feature described in feature10_decode_sep_hint.json, where a leading `sep=;` line sets the delimiter and is not emitted as data

context_pointers.vague_reference: encoding weirdness that makes spreadsheet files not open cleanly
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The `excel` preset (`preset: "excel"`) which sets fieldDelimiter to `;` and prepends a UTF-8 BOM (`\uFEFF`), defined in feature5_excel_encode.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: ask me if you're unsure what the rendering looks like for headers vs plain rows
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The `decode_headers` operation output format: `"<header>": <tag>=<json>` per field (feature13_headers.json), vs plain `decode` format: `<tag>=<json>` per field (feature6_decode_basic.json)

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect — you'll need to look at the existing test cases in `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` or the PRD in `start.md` for the precise behavior. Let me know if you have a more specific question about the business requirements.

feature10_decode_sep_hint.json.description: Decode CSV text that opens with a spreadsheet-style delimiter hint line of the form sep=<char>. When present, the hint sets the delimiter for the rest of the document and the hint line itself is not emitted as data; subsequent content is then split on that delimiter rather than any auto-detected one.

feature11_decode_skip_empty.json.description: Decode CSV text that contains blank lines, controlling whether they are kept. With empty-line skipping enabled (the default), rows that consist solely of empty fields are dropped. With skipping disabled, every physical line — including blank ones — becomes a row.

cases.expected_output:
rows=2
row[0]: str="A" | str="B"
row[1]: str="C" | str="D"

rows=4
row[0]: str="A" | str="B"
row[1]: str=""
row[2]: str="C" | str="D"
row[3]: str=""

feature12_dynamic_typing.json.description: Decode CSV text with automatic value typing enabled. Each field is examined and converted to an integer, floating-point number, or boolean when its full text matches that form; otherwise it stays textual. Boolean recognition is exact and case-sensitive, and even quoted fields are subject to typing. The reported neutral type tag reflects the inferred type of each field.

cases.expected_output:
rows=1
row[0]: int=123 | bool=true | str="hello" | double=1.5

feature13_headers.json.description: Decode CSV text while treating the first line as a header row, exposing each subsequent row by header name as well as by position. The result reports each data row as a mapping from header name to the corresponding typed field value, in column order.

cases.expected_output:
rows=2
row[0]: "id": str="1" | "name": str="Alice"
row[1]: "id": str="2" | "name": str="Bob"

feature14_multi_char_delim.json.description: Decode CSV text using an explicitly configured multi-character delimiter. The whole delimiter sequence separates fields, and single characters that merely begin the delimiter sequence are treated as ordinary content.

feature15_chunked_decode.json.description: Decode CSV text delivered as a sequence of arbitrary chunks, where row and field boundaries may be split across chunk edges. The decoder buffers partial content and reconstructs exactly the same rows it would produce from the concatenated text, emitting each completed row in order.

cases.expected_output:
rows=2
row[0]: str="A" | str="B" | str="C"
row[1]: str="d" | str="e" | str="f"

feature16_split_crlf.json.description: Decode chunked CSV text where a carriage-return/line-feed pair that ends a line is split so the carriage return ends one chunk and the line feed begins the next. The pair must be recognized as a single line terminator rather than producing a spurious empty row, regardless of the empty-line-skipping setting.

feature17_split_escape.json.description: Decode chunked CSV text containing an escaped quote sequence inside a quoted field, verifying correctness for every possible split point. The input is fed as two chunks for each split position from start to end, and every split must reconstruct the identical row, including the case where the escaped quote surrounds a delimiter that is therefore part of the field value.

cases.expected_output:
len=10
split[0]: [ str="a" | str="b\"c" | str="d" ]
split[1]: [ str="a" | str="b\"c" | str="d" ]
split[2]: [ str="a" | str="b\"c" | str="d" ]
split[3]: [ str="a" | str="b\"c" | str="d" ]
split[4]: [ str="a" | str="b\"c" | str="d" ]
split[5]: [ str="a" | str="b\"c" | str="d" ]
split[6]: [ str="a" | str="b\"c" | str="d" ]
split[7]: [ str="a" | str="b\"c" | str="d" ]
split[8]: [ str="a" | str="b\"c" | str="d" ]
split[9]: [ str="a" | str="b\"c" | str="d" ]
split[10]: [ str="a" | str="b\"c" | str="d" ]

feature18_split_multi_char.json.description: Decode chunked CSV text using a multi-character delimiter that is split across a chunk boundary, including the case where the split occurs immediately after a closing quote. The partial delimiter at a chunk edge must be buffered and rejoined so the fields separate correctly.

feature19_small_chunk_detect.json.description: Decode CSV text streamed one character at a time, forcing delimiter detection and the spreadsheet delimiter hint to work under maximal fragmentation. Even when no chunk individually contains enough context, the decoder buffers until it can determine the delimiter and then yields the same rows as a single-shot decode.

cases.expected_output:
rows=2
row[0]: str="a" | str="b" | str="c"
row[1]: str="1" | str="2" | str="3"

feature1_1_encode_join.json.description: Encode a list of rows into a single CSV text. Fields within a row are joined by the field delimiter and consecutive rows are separated by the line delimiter; no trailing line delimiter is appended after the final row.

feature20_streaming_encode.json.description: Encode rows fed one at a time through a streaming pipeline, where each row produces an output fragment and the concatenation of all fragments equals the full CSV text. The first row contributes no leading line delimiter and each later row is prefixed by the line delimiter.

feature21_codec_batching.json.description: Decode chunked CSV text through the batch-oriented codec adapter, where rows decoded from one input chunk are forwarded together as a single batch so natural chunk boundaries are preserved. Rows that straddle chunk edges (split fields, split line terminators, split escaped quotes, single-character feeding) are reconstructed correctly, and the number and contents of the emitted batches reflect how the input was chunked.

cases.expected_output:
batches=1
batch[0] rows=3: [ str="a" | str="b" ] [ str="1" | str="2" ] [ str="3" | str="4" ]

batches=2
batch[0] rows=1: [ str="a" | str="b" ]
batch[1] rows=1: [ str="c" | str="d" ]

feature22_codec_max_rows.json.description: Decode chunked CSV text through the batch-oriented codec adapter with a cap on the number of rows per emitted batch. A single chunk that decodes to more rows than the cap is split into multiple batches of at most that size, the cap composes with chunk boundaries and with split line terminators, and a cap larger than the available rows yields one batch.

cases.expected_output:
batches=3
batch[0] rows=1: [ str="a" | str="b" ]
batch[1] rows=1: [ str="c" | str="d" ]
batch[2] rows=1: [ str="e" | str="f" ]

batches=4
batch[0] rows=3: [ str="0" | str="row0" ] [ str="1" | str="row1" ] [ str="2" | str="row2" ]
batch[1] rows=3: [ str="3" | str="row3" ] [ str="4" | str="row4" ] [ str="5" | str="row5" ]
batch[2] rows=3: [ str="6" | str="row6" ] [ str="7" | str="row7" ] [ str="8" | str="row8" ]
batch[3] rows=1: [ str="9" | str="row9" ]

feature23_decoder_validation.json.description: Validate decoder configuration up front. The quote character and the escape character must each be exactly one character; supplying a multi-character value is rejected with a neutral configuration-error category identifying which option was invalid, while a valid single-character configuration is accepted.

feature2_encode_quoting.json.description: Encode rows where some fields require quoting. Under the default quoting policy a field is wrapped in the quote character only when it contains the field delimiter, a line break, or the quote character itself. An embedded quote character is escaped by doubling it (prefixing it with the escape character, which defaults to the quote character).

feature3_encode_quote_modes.json.description: Encode rows under explicit quoting policies other than the default. The 'strings' policy quotes only fields whose runtime value is textual, leaving numeric and boolean values unquoted. The 'always' policy quotes every field regardless of its type or content.

feature4_encode_escape_char.json.description: Encode using a custom escape character that differs from the quote character. When a quoted field contains the quote character, the configured escape character is placed before it instead of doubling the quote.

feature5_excel_encode.json.description: Encode using the spreadsheet-compatible preset: a semicolon field delimiter and a UTF-8 byte order mark prepended to the output. Fields that contain the semicolon delimiter are still quoted under the default necessary-quoting policy.

feature6_decode_basic.json.description: Decode CSV text into a list of rows of string fields. Fields are separated by the delimiter and rows by line breaks. Empty fields between consecutive delimiters are preserved as empty strings, and a single trailing line break does not create an extra empty row. Each decoded field is reported with a neutral type tag (here always textual) and its value.

cases.expected_output:
rows=1
row[0]: str="a" | str="b" | str="" | str="" | str="c" | str="d"

feature7_decode_quoted.json.description: Decode CSV text containing quoted fields. A field wrapped in the quote character may itself contain the delimiter, line breaks, or escaped quote characters (a doubled quote decodes to a single quote). Surrounding whitespace inside a quoted field is preserved, whitespace in an unquoted field is kept verbatim, a stray quote in the middle of an otherwise unquoted field is treated literally, and runs of escaped quotes around a delimiter decode correctly.

feature8_decode_autodetect.json.description: Decode CSV text without being told the delimiter, letting it be inferred from the content. The detector considers common candidates (comma, semicolon, tab, pipe) and picks the one that best and most consistently separates fields across the leading lines.

feature9_decode_bom.json.description: Decode CSV text that begins with a UTF-8 byte order mark. The byte order mark is consumed and never becomes part of the first field, whether the delimiter is supplied explicitly or inferred automatically.
